posts.title:
Minnesota’s SNAP error rate increased by 40%, new Federal Data shows
Starting in October of 2027, states with a SNAP error rate that exceeds 6% will be penalized. USDA says 12.58% of all Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) payments issued by the State of Minnesota Were Erroneous...

link_metadata.description: New data from the U.S. Department of Agriculture says 12.58% of all SNAP payments made by Minnesota were erroneous.

posts.author: mohel
posts.title: Rep. Shantel Brown just posted this. We are ruled by theater kids.

twitter.content: Rep. Shantel Brown just posted this. We are ruled by theater kids.

posts.raw_content:
As a disclaimer, these types of movies are not my cup of tea.  When I do watch them, I prefer a bit of cheesiness to lighten the mood as seen in John Wick or Jack Reacher, and at that point I'm only watching them out of respect for the actor.  This movie had none of that for me.

CV broke the genre entirely by being hyper political.  It was completely boring in that sense, but I couldn't stop watching.  Tropes were playing out that have been banned for seemingly decades which made me wonder if the discomfort from the film was simply due to my programming on how a story should unfold.

The overly flawed main character was refreshing when put into this context.  He was never meant to represent a hero, rather the resentment of comfort that ignored criminality.  An honest spectator with a moral compass that aligns with the philosophy that built western society.

The questions that dig at me after watching it are: why was it made, and why did Elon feel it so important to share?  There is an obvious guttural reaction from big accounts on why this is, and they well be correct, but what is the essence of this transaction?  I don't think most people watching this felt a call to violence, and I don't think that was intended due to the hyperbole of a hero that was delivered in the story.

To me, Elon bought the rights to post this to spread awareness in a lesser sense, but to rile the media in a greater sense.  This art, fanfic, imaginatory bullshit only swings one way, and it is whatever way the police and the media choose the wind to blow it.  Why should a film like this not exist when there is a clear audience for it, but any other violent fantasy should be a staple?

When you take away the violence, you get kids movies peddling the same themes that should not be broken, and it has been going on for far longer than the violent movies.

In short, I believe Elon bought the rights to this movie to expose the zombification of western society.
